journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Nursing Studies Advances

title-group.article-title: Measurement instruments in advance care planning: A systematic review of psychometric properties

contrib-group.aff: aFaculty of Psychology, University of Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain; Department of Social Psychology and Quantitative Psychology, University of Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain
bInstitute of Neurosciences, University of Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author at: Department of Social Psychology and Quantitative Psychology, Faculty of Psychology, University of Barcelona, Passeig de la Vall d’Hebrón, 171, 08035 Barcelona, Spain. mbarrios@ub.edu

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The use of accurate and targeted measurement instruments is critical for better understanding and supporting participation in advance care planning. However, it remains unclear which instruments are available and if they are psychometrically sound.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To systematically review existing measurement instruments within the framework of advance care planning and to evaluate their psychometric properties.

sec.title: Design
sec.p: Systematic review of measurement instruments.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A literature search was conducted in five databases (CINAHL, PsycInfo, PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science), from inception to January 2025. Studies aiming to develop, validate, or adapt a measurement instrument in advance care planning were included. The psychometric properties of reported instruments were thoroughly appraised using a consistent set of criteria, and those demonstrating the most robust properties for each population were highlighted.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 50 studies and 27 instruments were identified. Of these instruments, 14 (51.9 %) were designed for care recipients, 13 (48.1 %) for healthcare professionals, 4 (14.8 %) for caregivers, and 1 (3.7 %) for the general population. Notably, 5 instruments (18.5 %) were designed for use across multiple target populations. The instruments primarily assessed attitudes, readiness, and knowledge. Internal consistency was the most frequently examined psychometric property and generally demonstrated acceptable values. However, many studies did not assess dimensional structure, and among those that did, the results were often unsatisfactory. The Advance Care Planning Engagement Survey and the Advance Care Planning Self-Efficacy Scale emerged as the most promising instruments for assessing, respectively, care recipients’ engagement in advance care planning and healthcare professionals’ self-efficacy in implementing advance care planning.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This review provides a comprehensive reference for selecting psychometrically sound instruments suited to specific populations, measurement aspects, and health contexts. It highlights key gaps in the assessment of advance care planning, including the lack of tools for caregivers and the general population, limited cross-cultural validation, and insufficient psychometric support. By identifying these shortcomings, the review lays the groundwork for future research aiming at enhancing the evaluation and implementation of advance care planning practices.

sec.title: Registration
sec.p: Not registered.

list-item.p: Uptake rates for advance care planning vary widely across countries, with many reporting low participation, despite its positive impact.
Employing accurate and targeted measurement instruments is critical for better understanding and supporting participation in advance care planning.
Identifies instruments in advance care planning across key stakeholders.
Provides data on psychometric properties and guidance for instrument selection.
Highlights measurement gaps and suggests directions for future advance care planning research.

notes.p: Alt-text: Unlabelled box dummy alt text

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Advance care planning is an ongoing process that enables individuals to define their goals and preferences for future medical treatment and care, and it acquires particular importance when people are faced with challenges in communicating their wishes and preferences to others in situations where a response may be required (Rietjens et al., 2017). Advance care planning may be understood as an act of discussion and decision-making among patients, healthcare professionals, and caregivers with regard to future healthcare (Zhang et al., 2025). These three groups are recognized as the essential stakeholders of advance care planning in healthcare settings (Suen et al., 2024), and the content of their discussions may be documented in the form of written agreements or advance directives (Ng et al., 2022). The goal is to ensure that people receive medical care aligned with their values, goals, and preferences (Sudore et al., 2017b).

Advance care planning constitutes a critical component of medical care, and there is extensive evidence supporting its benefits across stakeholder groups. Among patients with heart failure, it has been shown to enhance quality of life, increase satisfaction with end-of-life care, and improve the quality of end-of-life communication (Schichtel et al., 2020). For individuals with malignant or chronic illnesses, research suggests that advance care planning effectively reduces decisional conflict, depression, and anxiety (Yeun, 2021). It also significantly improves concordance between patients’ end-of-life care preferences and the care received, and it is associated with improved bereavement outcomes, higher satisfaction with care quality and communication, and reduced decisional conflict among caregivers (Malhotra et al., 2024). Additionally, it has demonstrated positive effects on healthcare professionals by promoting wellbeing and facilitating more meaningful interactions with patients and their families (Gomes Souza et al., 2024). From a healthcare systems perspective, participation in advance care planning has been associated with decreased hospital admissions, fewer inpatient days, and lower medical care costs, thereby contributing to a more efficient and patient-centered care delivery system (Scott et al., 2024).

Recent data on uptake rates of advance care planning are lacking in many countries, particularly in Asia and Africa. Existing evidence indicates that engagement in advance care planning remains generally limited and at an early stage in Asia (Martina et al., 2021), and is rarely discussed or implemented in Africa (Basirika et al., 2023). Existing figures of Western world reveal considerable variation in uptake rates of advance care planning. One recent study reported that 64.7 % of older adults in Germany have completed advance care planning, followed by 44 % in the USA, 38.3 % in Switzerland, and 38.1 % in Canada, while only 5 % of older adults in Sweden, France, and Norway have done so (Ambade et al., 2024). These figures highlight a substantial gap between the potential benefits of advance care planning and its current implementation globally.

The implementation of advance care planning is influenced by multiple factors specific to each stakeholder group. Among healthcare professionals, key factors include attitudes and self-efficacy (Craig et al., 2020), knowledge (Cai et al., 2023), synchronized communication (Schichtel et al., 2021), and the presence of a team-based, coordinated care system (Toguri et al., 2020). For caregivers, readiness to engage in advance care planning (Pedrosa Carrasco et al., 2021), knowledge (Maragh-Bass et al., 2021), attitudes (Simon et al., 2015), and prior caregiving experience (Miyashita et al., 2021) are recognized as important factors. Similarly, patient-related factors include readiness (Pedrosa Carrasco et al., 2021), knowledge and confidence (Fenton et al., 2021), attitudes and past care experience (Simon et al., 2015), and individual care preferences (Yang et al., 2021). Suen et al. (2024) categorized these factors into three main groups: (1) intrapersonal factors, such as personal beliefs, emotions, perceived burden to others, and preference of timing; (2) interpersonal factors, including responsiveness of others, quality of relationship, family dynamics, previous care experience, and identity of the person to lead advance care planning discussion; and (3) socio-environmental factors, which encompass cultural characteristics and the availability of support systems. Research has also identified barriers to the implementation of advance care planning. Among patients, key barriers include limited awareness, the perception that advance care planning is not a priority, the belief that initiating such discussions is not their responsibility (Bernard et al., 2020), lack of knowledge, lack of ability to carry out advance care planning, and fear of discussing advance care planning (Poveda-Moral et al., 2021). For healthcare professionals, obstacles include poor interprofessional collaboration, limited time and resources, unclear role definitions, the absence of standardized documentation procedures (Marshall et al., 2024), lack of knowledge and skills to carry out advance care planning, and certain fear of starting advance care planning conversations (Poveda-Moral et al., 2021). As for family caregivers, who often act as surrogate decision-makers when patients are unable to make decisions, they frequently report feeling unprepared for this role and experiencing significant long-term emotional distress after making such decisions (Liu et al., 2024). The successful implementation of advance care planning requires efforts from all stakeholders involved. This highlights the need for standardized instruments, as they enable the rigorous and comparable assessment of the factors involved in the advance care planning process (Martínez Ques et al., 2022). This, in turn, supports the development of interventions that are more accurately tailored to the specific needs of each group, thereby fostering the effective implementation of advance care planning.

To date, however, no systematic review has provided a comprehensive overview of instruments available within the context of advance care planning for each of the stakeholder groups involved. Although a recent review by Jo et al. (2025) identified various tools used to assess patient-reported outcomes of advance care planning programs in older adults, instruments specifically developed within the framework of advance care planning for professionals, caregivers and diverse patient populations have yet to be identified. Accordingly, the present systematic review aims to identify such instruments, examine their key characteristics, and assess their psychometric properties.

sec.title: Literature search
sec.p: The search was conducted in January 2025 across five databases: CINAHL, PsycInfo, PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science. A combination of three categories of terms was used: (1) advance care planning-related terms adapted from Gaillard et al. (2023) (e.g., advance care planning, advance directives), (2) assessment-related terms derived from Felix et al. (2024) (e.g., instrument, scale), and (3) measurement properties based on Terwee et al. (2009) (e.g., validity, reliability). The search was limited to peer-reviewed articles, with no language or date restrictions being applied. For validation and adaptation studies that did not report details of the original development study, reference lists were reviewed to locate the latter. The full search strategy is available as supplementary material (Table S1).

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: Studies were included if they met the following criteria: they explicitly aimed to develop, validate or adapt a standardized instrument within the framework of advance care planning; and the study reported at least one psychometric property. Studies were excluded if the instrument did not have a specific name, following the criteria of Helbostad et al. (2009) and King et al. (2013), as lack of a clear identity might compromise transparency, traceability, and replicability.

sec.title: Selection process
sec.p: The selection process for the present systematic review followed the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines (Page et al., 2021).

After removing duplicates, 4768 articles were identified and uploaded to ASReview (https://asreview.nl/), an open-source machine learning tool for efficient and transparent systematic screening. Title and abstract screening were conducted in ASReview using the default naïve Bayes model and active learning, starting with a training set of 15 articles (i.e., 10 met the inclusion criteria and 5 did not). Screening was stopped after reviewing 20 % of records (n = 954) and reaching a 5 % threshold of consecutively irrelevant articles (n = 239), following the stopping rule described by Campos et al. (2024). Full texts of selected studies were retrieved and assessed, resulting in 46 articles that met the inclusion criteria. Four additional studies were included after locating missing development studies, yielding a final total of 50. Screening and selection were performed by a single reviewer, with any ambiguous cases resolved by consensus with an additional reviewer. The full process is shown in Fig. 1.Fig. 1
Flow chart showing the study identification and selection process.Fig. 1 dummy alt text

sec.title: Coding process
sec.p: Two categories of information were extracted: (1) General characteristics of the studies, including the type of study (i.e. development, validation or adaptation), sample size, respondents (i.e., individuals to whom the instrument was applied, including care recipients, which encompasses patients with specific health conditions and older adults, who constitute the primary users of advance care planning; healthcare professionals; caregivers, defined as individuals providing support during challenging circumstances, such as family members, partners or close friends; and the general population), respondents’ health condition, and the instrument’s name, its measurement aim, number of items, and language; and (2) Psychometric properties of the instruments, including evidence based on test content, dimensional structure, internal consistency, temporal stability, and relations to other variables.

Two researchers (CZ and AB) independently coded 62 % of the articles. In the event of disagreements, the coders re-examined the studies and discussed the differences in order to reach a consensus. If consensus could not be achieved, two additional researchers (MB and GG) were consulted to resolve the agreement. The inter-rater reliability of the coding process indicated almost perfect agreement, with a Cohen’s kappa of 0.977 (95 % CI [.971, 0.983]). The complete coding is available as supplementary material (Table S2).

sec.title: Appraisal of psychometric properties
sec.p: The appraisal of psychometric properties was conducted in accordance with the framework proposed by the Standards for Educational and Psychological Testing (American Educational Research Association, American Psychological Association, and National Council on Measurement in Education, 2014) and with specific criteria for assessing each psychometric property. Evidence based on test content was assessed following Polit et al. (2007), with thresholds for excellent validity set at an item-level content validity index (I-CVI) of ≥ 0.78 and a scale-level content validity index (S-CVI) of ≥ 0.90. Regarding the dimensional structure, the criterion of a factor solution explaining at least 60 % of the total variance was applied to determine satisfactory results in the exploratory factor analysis (EFA) (Hair et al., 2014). For the confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), model fit was evaluated using established indices: standardized root mean square residual (SRMR) values ≤ 0.08, root mean square error of approximation (RMSEA) ≤ 0.06, and comparative fit index (CFI) and Tucker–Lewis index (TLI) values ≥ 0.95, which indicate a reasonably good fit between the hypothesized model and the observed data (Brown, 2015). Internal consistency was assessed based on established criteria, and hence values ≥ 0.90 were considered ‘excellent,’ values ≥ 0.80 as ‘very good,’ values ≥ 0.70 as ‘adequate’, and values < 0.70 as ‘poor’ (Kline, 2016). Temporal stability was interpreted according to standard criteria, such that values ≥ 0.90 denoted excellent reliability, those ≥ 0.75 indicated good reliability, those ≥ 0.50 represented moderate reliability, and values < 0.50 were deemed poor (Koo and Li, 2016). Cohen’s criteria for correlation coefficients were employed to interpret the relations to other variables, with r ≥ 0.50 indicating a strong relationship, r ≥ 0.30 suggesting a moderate relationship, and r ≥ 0.10 representing a weak relationship (Cohen, 1988). In the case of instruments with multiple factors, internal consistency, temporal stability, and relations to other variables were evaluated based on the total score, if available. If not, the corresponding values for each subscale were averaged.

sec.title: Included studies
sec.p: The 50 included studies were published between 1997 and 2024. Of these, 24 (48 %) involved the development and validation of a new instrument, 20 (40 %) focused on the adaptation and validation of existing instruments, 4 (8 %) involved validation only, 1 (2 %) reported solely on instrument development, and 1 (2 %) encompassed the development, adaptation, and validation of a new instrument. The studies were considerably heterogeneous in terms of sample size, ranging from 10 to 1024 participants (M = 252.38, SD = 209.93). In 20 studies (40 %), the instrument in question was applied to specific health conditions. The majority of these studies (n = 12, 60 %) focused on participants with diverse chronic diseases, while the remaining studies addressed specific conditions (whether chronic or not) such as kidney disease, cardiovascular and metabolic diseases, and gynecologic cancer.
A detailed description of the characteristics of the included studies is provided as supplementary material (Table S3).

sec.title: Included instruments
sec.p: A total of 27 instruments were identified in this review. Of these, 7 (25.9 %) have multiple versions with varying numbers of items, and 6 (22.2 %) have been adapted for use in different languages. These instruments and their derivatives varied considerably in terms of measurement aim (e.g., attitudes, self-efficacy, communication, readiness), health conditions of the target populations (e.g., chronic disease, cancer, heart disease, kidney disease, mental health conditions), and the language of administration (e.g., English, Chinese, Korean, Japanese, Spanish).

Of the 27 instruments, 14 (51.9 %) were designed for care recipients, 13 (48.1 %) for professionals, 4 (14.8 %) for caregivers, and 1 (3.7 %) for the general population. Notably, 5 instruments (18.5 %) were intended for use with multiple target populations.
Detailed psychometric data for each instrument and its derived versions are provided as supplementary material (Table S4).

sec.title: Instruments designed for care recipients
sec.p: Among the 14 instruments designed for care recipients, the most common focus of measurement is readiness (n = 5), followed by attitudes (n = 3), self-efficacy (n = 2), preferences (n = 2), and knowledge (n = 2). Three instruments have multiple versions with varying numbers of items, and three have versions in different languages. Instruments demonstrating satisfactory dimensional structure, internal consistency, and temporal stability are highlighted below.

The Advance Care Planning Engagement Survey emerged as the most prominent in terms of both the number of psychometric studies (n = 10) and the overall quality of the psychometric evidence. Originally developed in English to assess care recipients’ engagement with advance care planning (Sudore et al., 2013), the instrument has since been adapted into Chinese (Liu et al., 2023, 2020; Wei et al., 2022), Japanese (Okada et al., 2021), Dutch (Van der Smissen et al., 2021), and Korean (Heo et al., 2024). The original instrument contained 49 items (Sudore et al., 2013), which were later expanded to 82 items and subsequently condensed into versions with 55, 34, 15, 9, and 4 items (Sudore et al., 2017a). In addition, Liu et al. (2020) adapted a 32-item Chinese version. The original 49-item instrument consists of two parts: ‘Process Measures’ (31 items, each rated on a 5-point Likert-type scale), encompassing four factors needed for people to engage in advance care planning behaviors (knowledge, contemplation, self-efficacy, and readiness); and ‘Action Measures’ (18 items with ‘yes’ or ‘no’ response options), assessing specific actions pertaining to each individual advance care planning behavior. Although the ‘Action Measures’ was removed in the shortened versions, “Moderate” to “Good” temporal stability was reported in the original version, with intraclass correlation (ICC) ranging from 0.57 to 0.81 (Sudore et al., 2013). EFA suggested that the instrument has a satisfactory factor structure (Liu et al., 2023; Okada et al., 2021; Sudore et al., 2017a), while CFA also indicated a good model fit (Heo et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023). All studies reported “Very good” to “Excellent” internal consistency, with α ranging from 0.80 to 0.97. Temporal stability ranged from “Moderate” to “Excellent”, with ICCs between 0.68 and 0.94, and Pearson correlation (r) of 0.85. Van der Smissen et al. (2021) reported a week correlation with general self-efficacy (r = 0.25), while Liu et al. (2020) observed a strong correlation with decisional conflict (r = 0.55).

Another instrument demonstrating strong psychometric potential is the Advance Care Planning Readiness Scale, developed in English to assess care recipients’ readiness to engage in advance care planning (Brown et al., 2017). The scale comprises 8 items rated on a 7-point Likert-type scale (1 = strongly disagree, 7 = strongly agree). Despite evidence of a satisfactory two-factor structure explaining 70 % of the variance, the model fit indices did not fully meet the convention thresholds for a good fit (CFI = 0.97, TLI = 0.96, RESEA = 0.096, SRMR = 0.07). Internal consistency was very good (α = 0.81), and the scale showed moderate temporal stability (r = 0.73). Additionally, scores on the instrument correlated moderately with acceptance of end-of-life care (r = 0.35), and showed a weak negative correlation with struggle with illness (r = −.28).

A similar Chinese version of the Advance Care Planning Readiness Scale, likewise developed to assess care recipients’ readiness to implement advance care planning, also demonstrated solid psychometric properties (Wang and Sheng, 2022). Comprising 22 items rated on a 5-point Likert-type scale (1 = totally disagree, 5 = totally agree), the instrument exhibited a satisfactory three-factor structure explaining 60 % of the variance (Wang and Sheng, 2022), and, in a subsequent study (Gao et al., 2024), good model fit (CFI = 0.992, TLI = 0.991, RMSEA = 0.023). Both studies reported excellent internal consistency (α = 0.92 and 0.93, respectively). However, temporal stability has yet to be assessed, and the instrument’s exclusive availability in Chinese currently limits its applicability across broader populations.

sec.title: Instruments designed for professionals
sec.p: Of the 13 instruments designed for healthcare professionals, three are focused on measuring attitudes, and two assess knowledge, preferences, and practice behaviors (e.g., initiate advance care planning discussions with patients). Four have multiple versions with varying numbers of items, and three have been adapted for use in different languages. Instruments demonstrating satisfactory dimensional structure, internal consistency, and temporal stability are highlighted below.

The Advance Care Planning Self-Efficacy Scale stood out in terms of the number of psychometric studies (n = 5) and quality of psychometric data. Originally developed in English to assess physician’s self-efficacy to implement advance care planning (Baughman et al., 2017), the instrument has since been adapted into Spanish (Di Gennaro et al., 2024; Lasmarías et al., 2021) and Chinese (Yang et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2024). While the original instrument comprises 17 items rated on a 5-point Likert-type scale (1 = not at all confident, 5 = very confident), the Spanish version includes 19 items and the Chinese versions contain 14 (Yang et al., 2022) and 17 items (Zhou et al., 2024). Yang et al. (2022) reported a satisfactory three-factor structure explaining 61.13 % of the variance, as well as good model fit (CFI = 0.974, TLI = 0.969, and RMSEA = 0.046). Zhou et al. (2024) reported a single-factor structure explaining 75.51 % of the variance, although model fit was not good (CFI = 0.893, TLI = 0.878, RMSEA = 0.141). Internal consistency in these studies was “Very good” to “Excellent”, with α ranging from 0.89 to 0.96, and temporal stability was “Good” to “Excellent”, with ICCs ranging from 0.88 to 0.98, and r of 0.94. Yang et al. (2022) reported a strong correlation with preferences (r = 0.89), while Zhou et al. (2024) found a strong correlation with general self-efficacy (r = 0.68). The Spanish version was reported to relate with several external variables, including a moderate correlation with the perceived ability to cope with difficult situations (r = 0.36), a weak correlation with attention to emotions (r = 0.12), a strong correlation with clarity of feelings (r = 0.50), a moderate correlation with mood repair (r = 0.33), and a strong correlation with professionals’ ability to cope with death (r = 0.56) (Lasmarías et al., 2021).

Another promising instrument is the PROPAD-pro (Martínez Ques et al., 2022), developed in Spanish to evaluate the quality of advance care planning practices (e.g., information, training, preferences, behavioral intention, and communication). Containing 18 items rated on a 5-point Likert-type scale, the scale has a satisfactory five-factor structure explaining 64.38 % of the variance. It showed very good internal consistency (α = 0.84) and good temporal stability (r = 0.79).

The Community Health Workers’ Willingness to Implement Advance Care Planning Questionnaire (Miao et al., 2022), developed in Chinese to assess willingness to implement advance care planning, also exhibited strong psychometric properties. The instrument comprises 30 items rated on a 5-point Likert-type scale (1 = strongly disagree, 5 = strongly agree), and it has a satisfactory five-factor structure explaining 74.96 % of the variance. Internal consistency was excellent overall (α = 0.97) and ranged from “Very good” to “Excellent” across factors (α = 0.87 - 0.95). Temporal stability was also good (r = 0.86).

sec.title: Instruments designed for caregivers
sec.p: The four instruments designed for caregivers measure various aspects: one focuses on engagement; another assesses knowledge, attitudes, and advance care planning practices; a third measures preferences; and the fourth evaluates knowledge, attitudes, behavioral intentions, factors influencing advance care planning, and subjective norms. Most of these instruments lack comprehensive psychometric evaluation, the only exception being the 17-item Advance Care Planning Engagement Survey (Van Scoy et al., 2019), aimed at measuring caregiver engagement in advance care planning and adapted from the instrument originally designed for care recipients (Sudore et al., 2013). The Advance Care Planning Engagement Survey for caregivers yielded a satisfactory three-factor structure explaining 91 % of the variance, and it showed excellent internal consistency (α = 0.91).

Table 3 presents a summary of the characteristics and psychometric properties of the instruments designed for caregivers.Table 3

Advance Care Planning Engagement Survey
Van Scoy et al. (2019)Engagement17English-Satisfactory-Excellent--

Engagement9English---ExcellentExcellent-

Note. #: number; EFA: exploratory factor analysis; CFA: confirmatory factor analysis; ACP: advance care planning; -: not tested; NR: not reported.

sec.title: Instruments designed for the general population
sec.p: Only one instrument was identified as being specifically designed for use with the general population: the General Public’s Attitudes Toward Advance Care Directives (Laranjeira et al., 2021). Developed in Portuguese to assess the general public’s attitudes toward advance care planning, the instrument comprises 26 items rated on a 5-point Likert-type scale (1 = strongly disagree, 5 = strongly agree). Although a five-factor structure was proposed, it accounted for just 50.06 % of the total variance, suggesting a suboptimal factorial solution. Nevertheless, the instrument demonstrated very good internal consistency (α = 0.85). Temporal stability has not yet been assessed.

sec.title: Main findings
sec.p: This systematic review provides an overview of existing measurement instruments for use in the context of advance care planning, considering both what they assess and their psychometric properties. A total of 27 instruments targeted at four populations (i.e., care recipients, healthcare professionals, caregivers, and the general population) were identified.

In order to evaluate the set of available tools aimed at assessing the advance care planning process, it is necessary to consider the factors that may influence its implementation. As indicated by previous research, lack of knowledge (Poveda-Moral et al., 2021), readiness (Tan et al., 2024), personal beliefs, previous care experience, and cultural characteristics (Suen et al., 2024) are all critical factors influencing the implementation of advance care planning. To gain a more comprehensive understanding of advance care planning implementation, it is therefore essential to assess the aforementioned aspects across all the stakeholders involved. Our results indicate that the available instruments for care recipients commonly assess readiness, self-efficacy, attitudes, and knowledge, while factors such as emotions and quality of relationships with caregivers and healthcare professionals have received less attention. Instruments designed for healthcare professionals are mainly focused on attitudes, knowledge, preferences, and practice behaviors such as initiating advance care planning discussions with patients, whereas self-efficacy, despite its recognized importance in facilitating advance care planning, has been less thoroughly addressed (Baughman et al., 2017). As for caregivers and the general population, the notable lack of validated instruments specifically designed for these two stakeholder groups limits the ability to draw firm conclusions about the gap in assessment.

A key aim of the present review was to thoroughly appraise the psychometric properties of the identified instruments. Considering all the instruments available across all stakeholder groups, our data show that internal consistency is the most frequently evaluated property, generally yielding acceptable values. This is followed by evaluations of dimensional structure, which often do not indicate satisfactory solutions, and content validity, the evidence for which is strong for most instruments. Temporal stability has received comparatively less attention, though results are generally acceptable. Evidence based on relations to other variables is particularly limited. While many instruments lack comprehensive validation, we have highlighted those demonstrating strong psychometric properties. However, since we used a consistent set of criteria to appraise each psychometric property, our conclusions may differ from those in the original studies.

Among instruments targeting care recipients, the Advance Care Planning Engagement Survey (Sudore et al., 2013), designed for measuring engagement with advance care planning, is the one that has been most extensively validated, with a substantial number of studies providing evidence of its robust psychometric properties. Assessing engagement is particularly crucial as it is closely linked to behavioral change models in healthcare and has been shown to predict advance care planning initiation and completion (Sudore et al., 2013). The availability of the Advance Care Planning Engagement Survey in multiple languages enhances its global applicability in this respect. Another notable instrument for care recipients is the Chinese Advance Care Planning Readiness Scale (Wang and Sheng, 2022), which focuses on assessing individuals’ readiness to engage in advance care planning. While the Chinese Advance Care Planning Readiness Scale demonstrates promising psychometric properties, its exclusive availability in Chinese constrains its cross-cultural applicability.

For healthcare professionals, the Advance Care Planning Self-Efficacy Scale (Baughman et al., 2017), designed to assess self-efficacy, emerged as the most prominent instrument, both in terms of the number of psychometric evaluations and the strength of psychometric evidence. Moreover, its availability in English, Chinese, and Spanish (languages spoken by an estimated 2.25 billion individuals worldwide) facilitates cross-cultural applicability and inclusivity. Other instruments in this category include the PROPAD-pro (Martínez Ques et al., 2022), designed to measure the perceived quality of advance care planning, and the Community Health Workers’ Willingness to Implement Advance Care Planning Questionnaire (Miao et al., 2022), designed to measure willingness to implement advance care planning. Both have demonstrated sound psychometric properties, although their availability in a single language (Spanish and Chinese, respectively) restrict their international utility.

Instruments targeting caregivers remain limited. Nevertheless, the adapted version of the Advance Care Planning Engagement Scale for measuring caregiver engagement with advance care planning (Van Scoy et al., 2019) showed encouraging psychometric properties, suggesting potential for further refinement and validation in this underrepresented population. Regarding the general population, the only tool identified was the General Public’s Attitudes Toward Advance Care Directives (Laranjeira et al., 2021), designed to measure attitudes toward advance care planning. Despite its relevance, it lacks comprehensive psychometric evaluation, indicating a need for further research.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: One potential limitation of this review lies in the inclusion criterion, whereby we focused exclusively on studies explicitly aiming to develop, validate, or adapt instruments within the context of advance care planning. Consequently, instruments initially developed for other purposes but later applied in advance care planning-related research may not have been captured. A second limitation is the exclusion of grey literature, such as theses, conference proceedings, and non-peer-reviewed reports, which may have limited the comprehensiveness of the instrument pool, particularly for emerging tools not yet formally published. Additionally, the databases we used primarily index journals that publish in English, which may limit the retrieval of non-English publications. A further limitation of this review is the absence of a formal risk of bias assessment. Although the Consensus-based Standards for the selection of health Measurement Instruments (COSMIN) checklist exist to evaluate the methodological quality of studies on patient-reported outcome measures, its terminology and criteria are mainly suited to health-related self-report instruments. Given that the present review included instruments for care recipients, healthcare professionals, caregivers, and general population, the existing tool was not fully aligned with the scope of this study. Moreover, although we used recognized psychometric criteria when evaluating studies, the focus was on the presence or absence of specific properties rather than the methodological rigor with which they were evaluated. It should also be noted that some instruments were developed fairly recently, and hence validation evidence is still emerging. Lastly, while the use of ASReview enhanced screening efficiency, its performance relies heavily on the quality and consistency of the initial labeled data (Chan et al., 2024). Early labeling bias may influence the algorithm’s prioritization, and the tool functions as a black-box model, offering limited transparency in its decision-making process (Quan et al., 2024).

sec.title: Implications and future directions
sec.p: Despite its demonstrated clinical benefits, the implementation of advance care planning remains limited in many Western countries (Ambade et al., 2024), is still in its early stages in Asia (Martina et al., 2021), and is rarely discussed or implemented in Africa (Basirika et al., 2023). Sound measurement constitutes a fundamental prerequisite for generating meaningful knowledge and fostering theoretical and practical progress, as it enables systematic improvement of what under investigation (Rammstedt and Bluemke, 2019). A key barrier to the promotion of advance care planning practice and policy lies in the absence of standardized, psychometrically robust tools capable of capturing the complexity of stakeholders’ engagement, attitudes, knowledge, and behavioral intentions. Consequently, the accurate assessment of advance care planning outcomes requires the use of valid and reliable instruments tailored to specific populations and settings (Van der Smissen et al., 2021). By identifying and appraising validated instruments across care recipients, healthcare professionals, and caregivers, this systematic review provides a critical resource for clinicians, researchers, and policymakers seeking to monitor, compare, and improve advance care planning practices. In doing so, it may contribute to more consistent and evidence-based evaluations of advance care planning implementation. This is important, because the use of appropriate measurement instruments can help align care more closely with individuals’ values and preferences, inform the design of targeted interventions, and guide policy decisions aimed at increasing advance care planning uptake and equity. Furthermore, the availability of validated instruments to measure advance care planning-related aspects would not only facilitate the assessment of its benefits for different stakeholders (care recipients, caregivers, and healthcare professionals), but also enable the evaluation of its economic implications within healthcare systems, such as potential cost savings, resource optimization, and improved alignment of care with patient preferences (Dixon et al., 2015). Moreover, validated instruments can serve as valuable tools for quality improvement and training within hospice and palliative settings, helping services to identify gaps in staff knowledge or confidence, evaluate intervention effectiveness, and demonstrate outcomes to funders and policymakers (Soikkeli-Jalonen et al., 2020).

Several avenues for future research emerge from the findings of this review. First, there is still a lack of instruments addressing specific aspects of advance care planning, in particular, emotions and quality of relationships with caregivers and healthcare professionals for care recipients, self-efficacy for professionals, and a broad range of aspects for caregivers and the general population, for whom few instruments are available. Caregivers and general population may approach advance care planning from different perspectives, influenced by distinct roles, levels of knowledge, emotional involvement, and contexts outside clinical environments. This highlights the need for instruments specifically tailored or validated for these groups to ensure comprehensive and contextually accurate measurement of advance care planning-related aspects such as readiness, attitudes, knowledge, or engagement. Second, many existing instruments lack robust psychometric validation, underscoring the importance of further studies focused on gathering validity and reliability evidence. Third, although some instruments exhibit strong psychometric properties, their lack of availability in different languages constrains their use globally. Cross-cultural adaptation efforts are therefore needed to enhance the applicability of these tools across diverse settings. Finally, developing instruments for specific health areas, such as mental health, could address the unique challenges experienced by care recipients, caregivers, and professionals in these contexts (Monnet et al., 2024). Such tools would enable a more nuanced understanding of person-centered preferences in varied clinical environments, contributing to ethical and inclusive care planning.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The study was supported by the Ministry of Science and Innovation of Spain - MCIN/ AEI /10.13039/501100011033/ (grant PID2022–137108OB-I00); and the Agency for Management of University and Research Grants (AGAUR) of the Government of Catalonia (grant number 2021SGR01071).

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Chao Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Maite Barrios: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ángela I. Berrío: Formal analysis, Data curation. Georgina Guilera: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
